questions.question: What does the term "continence" mean?
options: Needing to use the toilet more often at night
Being able to control when and where you pass urine and stool
Any involuntary leaking of urine or stool
Always requiring pads to manage the bladder

questions.question: What does "incontinence" mean?
options: A normal, unavoidable part of getting older
Any involuntary leaking of urine or stool
Being able to hold urine until reaching a toilet
Drinking too little fluid across the day

questions.question: Why should incontinence never be treated as simply a practical problem to be managed?
options: Because it only matters if the person mentions it themselves
Because pads are the only support anyone ever needs
Because it affects a person's dignity, self-esteem and wider health, and can often be improved with the right support
Because it is a normal, unavoidable part of getting older

questions.question: A person leaks a small amount of urine each time they cough, sneeze or laugh. Which type of incontinence does this describe?
options: Overflow incontinence
Functional incontinence
Urge incontinence
Stress incontinence

questions.question: Which type of incontinence involves a sudden, intense need to pass urine, followed by leaking before the person can reach a toilet?
options: Stress incontinence
Overflow incontinence
Functional incontinence
Urge incontinence

questions.question: A man passes frequent small dribbles, feels his bladder is never quite empty, and strains to pass urine. Which type of incontinence does this best describe?
options: Functional incontinence
Overflow incontinence
Stress incontinence
Urge incontinence

questions.question: A person's bladder and bowel work normally, but poor mobility and confusion mean they cannot reach or use the toilet in time. Which type of incontinence is this?
options: Functional incontinence
Urge incontinence
Overflow incontinence
Stress incontinence

questions.question: Who carries out a continence assessment and prescribes continence products?
options: The person's family or next of kin
Any care worker who knows the person's routine
Whoever is nearest when an accident happens
A nurse or continence advisor

questions.question: Which of the following best supports a person to stay continent and dignified?
options: Leaving the toilet door open and hurrying the person along
Knowing their usual routine, responding to requests promptly, and keeping the route to the toilet clear and easy to use
Waiting until an accident happens before offering any help
Encouraging the person to drink very little so they need the toilet less

questions.question: Unless a person's care plan says otherwise, roughly how much should most adults be encouraged to drink across the day?
options: As little as possible to avoid accidents
Only water first thing in the morning
At least 5 litres
Around 1.5 to 2 litres

questions.question: What happens when a person drinks too little fluid?
options: Their urine becomes concentrated, which can irritate the bladder and raise the risk of infection and constipation
Their skin becomes better protected from damage
Their bladder muscles become stronger over time
Their continence always improves

questions.question: How should continence products be used within a person's care?
options: As one part of a plan that still promotes independence, with the right product used privately and changed promptly
By choosing whatever pad is nearest, regardless of fit or assessment
By leaving them in place as long as possible to save changes
As a replacement for helping the person get to the toilet

questions.question: Why does prompt changing and keeping skin clean and dry matter so much in continence care?
options: Because urine and stool irritate the skin, which can become red, sore and broken if not managed
Because it allows one pad to be used for the whole day
Because it means the person can be given fewer drinks
Because it removes the need for any continence assessment

questions.question: How are continence and pressure damage connected?
options: They are completely unrelated to one another
Keeping skin damp actually protects it from pressure damage
Skin that is repeatedly wet from urine or stool becomes more fragile and can combine with pressure to cause pressure ulcers
Pressure ulcers only affect people who are fully continent

questions.question: You notice an older person has become suddenly confused, and their urine is cloudy and strong-smelling. What should you do?
options: Assume the confusion is just part of getting older and do nothing
Start the person on antibiotics yourself
Report it promptly to a senior colleague or nurse and record what you have observed, as these can be signs of a UTI
Cut back their fluids to reduce how often they use the toilet

questions.question: Which of the following can be a sign of constipation?
options: Leaking urine when laughing or coughing
A burning feeling when passing urine
Cloudy, strong-smelling urine
Going to the toilet less often than usual, with hard or painful stools

questions.question: When should you involve a nurse or continence advisor?
options: Only if the person specifically asks you to
Only once a problem has become severe and cannot be ignored
Never, because care workers should manage all continence problems alone
When someone develops new incontinence, their pattern changes, or you see signs of infection, constipation or skin damage

questions.question: What is the care worker's role in continence care?
options: To follow the agreed care plan, promote independence, and observe and report changes
To diagnose and treat any infections that arise
To carry out the continence assessment and prescribe products
To decide when a person needs to start wearing pads

questions.question: Stress incontinence is linked to which of the following?
options: Weakened pelvic floor muscles, which can follow childbirth, ageing or some surgery
An enlarged prostate blocking the bladder
An overactive bladder made worse by caffeine
Not being able to find or reach the toilet in time

questions.question: Overflow incontinence can be linked to which of the following?
options: A blockage or, in men, an enlarged prostate
Clothing that is hard to undo
Weakened pelvic floor muscles after childbirth
Drinking caffeine late in the day

questions.question: Which of these is a possible cause of functional incontinence?
options: Poor mobility, pain, poor eyesight, confusion, or clothing that is hard to undo
An overactive bladder
A bladder that cannot empty fully
Weakened pelvic floor muscles

questions.question: Why should incontinence be understood through a proper assessment rather than an assumption?
options: Because assessment is only needed for people living with dementia
Because guessing the cause or simply reaching for pads can miss a treatable problem
Because all types of incontinence are supported in exactly the same way
Because it removes the need for the care worker to report anything

questions.question: The course stresses that continence care should never be seen as "just pads". What does this mean?
options: Care workers should choose their own products for each person
Continence care is skilled, dignified work, and products are only one part of good support
Pads should never be used under any circumstances
Only nurses are allowed to touch continence products

questions.question: How can drinks such as caffeine affect some people's continence?
options: They make the bladder empty more completely
They prevent urinary infections
They can worsen urgency and make urge incontinence worse
They strengthen the pelvic floor muscles

questions.question: When supporting someone with a continence product, which approach protects their dignity?
options: Leaving the product in place as long as possible to reduce changes
Explaining what you are doing, keeping the person covered, working gently, and not making comments that could embarrass them
Commenting on any smell so the person knows to be more careful
Working quickly in an open doorway to save time

questions.question: If someone in your care starts having accidents, it is fine for you to decide on your own that they need to start wearing continence pads.
options: True
False

questions.question: Incontinence is a normal, unavoidable part of getting older that people simply have to put up with.
options: True
False

questions.question: Constipation can make bladder problems worse and can sometimes cause loose stool to leak, so changes in someone's bowel habits should be reported.
options: True
False

questions.question: Encouraging a person to drink much less is a good way to reduce accidents and improve their continence.
options: True
False

questions.question: In older people especially, a sudden change such as new confusion or agitation can be an important early sign of a urinary tract infection.
options: True
False

questions.question: A wet or soiled pad left in place quickly makes the skin sore and undermines the person's dignity.
options: True
False

questions.question: If you suspect a person has a urinary tract infection, it is part of your role to diagnose it and start them on treatment yourself.
options: True
False

questions.question: Good continence care and good skin care always go together.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following can be signs of a urinary tract infection (UTI)? (Select all that apply)
options: Leaking a small amount of urine when coughing or sneezing
An improved appetite and more energy than usual
Pain or a burning feeling when passing urine
Cloudy, dark or strong-smelling urine
New or worse confusion in an older person

questions.question: Which of the following are types of incontinence described in this course? (Select all that apply)
options: Caffeine incontinence
Urge incontinence
Functional incontinence
Overflow incontinence
Stress incontinence

questions.question: Which actions help make the toilet easier for a person to reach and use in time? (Select all that apply)
options: Choosing clothing with easier fastenings
Making sure walking aids are within reach
Keeping the way to the toilet clear and well lit
Using signs or a clear line of sight to help someone with dementia find the toilet
Encouraging the person to drink very little

questions.question: Which of the following can be signs of constipation? (Select all that apply)
options: Going to the toilet less often than usual
Cloudy, strong-smelling urine
Tummy discomfort and a poor appetite
Straining, or hard or painful stools

questions.question: Which of these are good practice when supporting someone with a continence product? (Select all that apply)
options: Explaining what you are doing and keeping the person covered
Checking products regularly and changing them promptly when needed
Making comments about the product that could cause embarrassment
Switching the person to a different product yourself if one seems wrong
Handling used products hygienically and disposing of them correctly

questions.question: When should you report to a nurse or continence advisor? (Select all that apply)
options: When their skin becomes red, sore or broken
When their usual pattern changes
When someone develops new incontinence
Only after a problem has already become severe
When you see signs of infection or constipation

questions.question: Which of the following are good practice for skin care in continence support? (Select all that apply)
options: Looking at the skin each time and reporting redness or soreness straight away
Applying any barrier cream that has been prescribed
Leaving the skin in contact with a wet product to save time
Patting rather than rubbing the skin dry
Cleaning the skin gently with plain water or a mild product as set out in the care plan

questions.question: Leaking urine when you cough or sneeze is called ___ incontinence.
A sudden, desperate need to pass urine that is hard to hold is called ___ incontinence.
When the bladder never empties fully and leaks or dribbles, this is called ___ incontinence.
When someone cannot reach the toilet in time because of mobility or memory, it is called ___ incontinence.
Drinking too little makes urine ___ and can irritate the bladder.
